sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of right knee pain following a fall from a tandem bike.
He reports that he hit his knee on the curb and took some ibuprofen, which provided temporary relief.
However, the pain has persisted and worsened with activities such as walking uphill.
He denies any previous knee problems and has been taking ibuprofen as needed.
Past medical history: The patient works as a baby rocker at a children's hospital and enjoys walking his dog.
He is physically active and has played sports in high school, but has not been involved in any athletic activities since then.
He denies any history of smoking, alcohol, or drug use.
Review of systems: The patient reports pain in the right knee, particularly with weight-bearing activities, but denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: Ibuprofen as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's knee was tender to palpation on the medial joint line, with a small abrasion on the medial patella.
The patient had a slight laxity in the posterior drawer test and a positive one-plus posterior drawer sign.
The lateral collateral ligament and medial collateral ligament were intact.
The patient had full extension and flexion in both knees, with no deformities or crepitus.
Test Results: X-rays of the knee were reviewed, which showed no obvious fractures or bone spurs, but the doctor suspects a possible injury to the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL).
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a suspected injury to the posterior cruciate ligament of the right knee, likely due to a fall from a tandem bike.
Problem list: Knee pain; suspected posterior cruciate ligament injury; right knee instability; possible meniscus tear or chipped cartilage.
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen 400mg three times a day for pain management.
I recommended ice packs a few times a day to reduce swelling.
I will send a prescription for a different medication that may be easier to take, which the patient can take twice a day.
I will get the patient a referral to physical therapy to improve strength and motion.
I will also get a brace fitted to the patient's knee for temporary support.
I will re-evaluate the patient in 4-6 weeks to assess progress.
Follow-up:
The patient will follow up in 4-6 weeks to re-evaluate the knee and assess for any need for further treatment, including possible surgery to repair the PCL.
